title: Browser Agents
lesson:
Most software talks to other software through tidy, pre-agreed channels called APIs (an API is an official "side door" a service opens for programs to plug into). But huge chunks of the world have no such door. Booking a niche hotel, filling a government form, checking a supplier portal, pulling a report from an old internal dashboard — these live only behind a normal web page meant for human eyes and a mouse. A browser agent is an AI Agent that drives a real web browser the way a person would: it looks at the page, decides where to click or type, does it, looks again, and keeps going until the task is done.

Analogy 1: a remote-controlled intern

Picture hiring an intern who works from home and shares their screen. You say "refund the order from Tuesday." They open the site, squint at the screen, find the orders tab, click it, scroll, spot Tuesday's order, hit refund, confirm. You never tell them the exact pixel to click — they figure it out from what they see. A browser agent is that intern, except the "eyes" are a model reading the page and the "hand" is code that controls the browser.

What the agent actually "sees"

A browser agent runs in a loop, and the loop is the whole idea. Each turn it gathers the current state of the page, sends that to the model, gets back one action, performs it, and repeats. There are two main ways to show the page to the model:

  • Screenshots (pixels). The agent sends an image of the page to a VLM (a vision-language model — one that can read pictures, not just text). The model literally looks at the screen and says "click the blue Submit button at the bottom." This works on any site, even ones built as a wall of images.
  • Accessibility tree / HTML (text). Instead of a picture, the agent hands the model a cleaned-up text outline of the page: every button, link, and field, each with a number. The model replies "click element 14." This is cheaper, faster, and less error-prone than guessing pixel coordinates.

Many real systems do both: a text outline for precision, plus a screenshot when the layout is confusing. Either way, the model never controls the browser directly. It only emits actions — click, type, scroll, navigate — which is just Tool Calling aimed at a browser instead of an API.

The perceive–decide–act loop

Here is the cycle in plain pseudo-code:

goal = "Find the cheapest flight to Lisbon next Friday"
state = browser.snapshot()        # screenshot + labelled elements
while not done:
    action = model.decide(goal, state, history)
    # e.g. {"type": "click", "element_id": 23}
    browser.do(action)
    state = browser.snapshot()     # look again after acting
    done = model.is_goal_met(goal, state)

This "look again after every action" step is what separates an agent from a fixed script. A normal automation script breaks the moment a button moves. The agent re-reads the page each turn, so it can adapt when the site looks different than expected. That makes it a kind of Agentic Workflow where the environment is a live web page.

Analogy 2: GPS that recalculates

An old paper map gives you one fixed route; miss a turn and you are lost. A satnav recalculates after every wrong turn because it keeps checking where you actually are. A hard-coded macro is the paper map. A browser agent is the satnav: it keeps re-checking the page and re-planning, which is why it survives pop-ups, cookie banners, and redesigned layouts that would shatter a brittle script.

Where this matters in real products

By 2026 this is a hot area: providers ship hosted "computer use" browser agents, and open tools like Playwright-based frameworks let you build your own. Real uses include automated QA testing of websites, scraping data from sites with no API, filling repetitive forms, comparison shopping, and onboarding flows that touch five different legacy portals. It overlaps heavily with AI Automation, and a complex job is often split across several agents (a Multi-Agent System) — one to plan, one to drive the browser, one to check the result.

⚠️ Browser agents are powerful and risky: a wrong click can buy the wrong thing, submit a real form, or delete data. Always test in a throwaway account, and for anything that spends money or changes records, keep a human approval step in the loop.

The honest current state: browser agents are genuinely useful but still flaky. They get lost on dense pages, misread tiny buttons, and slow tasks down because every step is a model call. Treat them as a capable-but-clumsy intern who needs guardrails, not a flawless robot.
mentalModel: A browser agent is a remote intern sharing your screen: each turn it looks at the page, picks one action (click/type/scroll), does it, then looks again — a perceive–decide–act loop that re-checks reality after every move, like a satnav recalculating.
mistakes: Confusing it with a fixed script. A recorded macro replays the exact same clicks and shatters when a button moves. A browser agent re-reads the page each turn and adapts. If you wanted rock-solid repeatability on a stable site, a plain script is cheaper and more reliable — reach for an agent only when the page changes or needs judgement.

Letting it run with no guardrails on real accounts. The model can hallucinate a click and end up buying something, sending an email, or deleting a record. Always start in a sandbox or test account, and gate any irreversible action (payments, submissions, deletions) behind a human approval step.

Feeding it the raw, full HTML. A modern page is tens of thousands of tokens of nested tags and scripts. Dumping all of it blows the context window and confuses the model. Send a cleaned outline (the accessibility tree) of just the interactive elements instead.

Relying on pixel coordinates alone. Telling the model to 'click at x=412, y=830' is fragile — it misses by a few pixels, screens resize, layouts shift. Prefer labelling elements with stable IDs so the action is 'click element 14', not a guess at where it sits.

No step limit or loop guard. Agents can get stuck clicking the same dead button forever, burning money and time. Always cap the number of steps, detect when the page hasn't changed after an action, and bail out or ask for help instead of looping.

Ignoring how slow and expensive it is. Every single step is a model call plus a page load, so a ten-action task can take a minute and cost real tokens. Don't use a browser agent for something a simple API call or script could do in 200 milliseconds.
exercise:
Sketch (on paper or in pseudo-code) the perceive–decide–act loop for one concrete task: "Go to a public library catalogue site and check whether a specific book is available." Write out, step by step, what the agent sees and what single action it takes each turn — for example: turn 1 sees the homepage, action = type the title into the search box; turn 2 sees results, action = click the matching book; turn 3 sees the book page, action = read the availability status; done. Note where the loop checks 'is the goal met yet?'

Stretch goal: Add three guardrails to your design: a maximum step count, a rule for what the agent does if the same page appears twice in a row (it's stuck), and a human-approval checkpoint — then explain which one real-world failure each guardrail prevents.
solution: A clean loop looks like: snapshot → model picks one action → browser does it → snapshot again → is goal met? repeated until done. For the library task: (1) homepage → type title in search; (2) results list → click the matching title; (3) book page → read 'Available / On loan'; goal met, stop and report. The key insight is that the agent re-snapshots after every action, so it reacts to the real new page rather than what it assumed would appear. Guardrails: a step cap (say 15) stops infinite loops that burn tokens; a 'same page twice → stuck' rule catches a button that does nothing so the agent asks for help instead of spinning; and a human-approval checkpoint before any irreversible click (placing a hold, paying a fine) prevents the agent from taking a real, costly action on a wrong guess. Together they turn a clumsy intern into a safe one.
summary: A browser agent is an AI that drives a real web browser like a person — useful precisely when a site has no API. It runs a perceive–decide–act loop: look at the page (as a screenshot for a VLM, or as a labelled text outline), pick one action (click/type/scroll/navigate via tool calling), do it, then look again. That re-checking is what lets it adapt where rigid scripts break. As of 2026 they are powerful but flaky and slow, so cap the steps, prefer element IDs over pixels, and keep a human in the loop for anything irreversible.

keyTerms.def: An AI agent that controls a real web browser — clicking, typing, scrolling, navigating — to complete tasks on websites the way a human would.
The core cycle of an agent: capture the current page state, send it to the model for one action, perform that action, then capture the page again and repeat.
Application Programming Interface — a structured 'side door' a service exposes so programs can talk to it directly. Browser agents are for sites that have no such door.
A cleaned-up text outline of a web page listing its interactive elements (buttons, links, fields), often with ID numbers, given to the model instead of raw HTML or pixels.
A model that can read images as well as text, letting a browser agent literally 'look at' a screenshot of the page to decide what to click.
The broader capability (and the name some providers use) for an AI controlling a screen, mouse, and keyboard — driving a browser is the most common form.
A real browser running without a visible window, controlled by code (e.g. via Playwright). Browser agents usually drive one of these behind the scenes.
A safety limit on an agent — a step cap, a stuck-detector, or a human-approval gate before irreversible actions like payments or deletions.
A stable label attached to a page element so the agent can say 'click element 14' instead of guessing fragile pixel coordinates.
